question: A patient with a known primary immunodeficiency developed progressive headaches, visual blurring, and left-sided weakness and paresthesia over 4 months. Brain MRI was performed (image provided). Which of the following diagnostic tests is most appropriate next to establish the suspected etiology?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the MRI image: an axial FLAIR-type sequence shows multiple asymmetric, confluent hyperintense lesions in the subcortical and deep white matter, most prominent in the right temporoparietal region and the posterior hemispheres. These lesions are non-focal, involve white matter rather than a single mass, and correspond anatomically to the patient’s symptoms (right-sided temporoparietal disease can account for the patient’s left-sided weakness/paresthesia and posterior involvement can explain visual blurring).

Next, I relate these imaging and clinical features to the question, which asks what test would best establish the suspected etiology in a patient with known primary immunodeficiency and progressive multifocal white matter disease. The clinical context (immunodeficiency) plus multifocal white-matter lesions raises strong concern for an opportunistic viral leukoencephalopathy caused by JC virus (progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy).

Then, I consider how each option aligns with that working diagnosis: CSF JC virus PCR (A) is a minimally invasive test that can demonstrate the viral etiology and so directly establish the suspected diagnosis. Stereotactic brain biopsy (B) is invasive and typically reserved only if less invasive tests (like CSF studies) are non-diagnostic. Contrast-enhanced MRI with DWI (C) might provide additional anatomic detail but would not by itself identify a viral cause. A CSF autoimmune/paraneoplastic panel (D) targets an autoimmune etiology, which is less likely here given the immunodeficiency and the imaging pattern suggesting an opportunistic infectious process.

Finally, tying these observations together: in this immunodeficient patient with progressive multifocal white-matter lesions on MRI, the next most appropriate test to establish the suspected JC virus etiology is CSF JC virus PCR.

image_context: About 4 months ago, the patient was first diagnosed with nausea and headache, which gradually worsened along with progressive blurred vision, paresis, and paresthesia on the left side of the body. On brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), subcortical lesions in the left occipital and right temporoparietal area (Fig. <xref rid="13256_2022_3333_Fig1_HTML" ref-type="fig">1</xref>), as well as the involvement of parts of the corpus callosum and thalamus (Fig. ), as well as the involvement of parts of the corpus callosum and thalamus (Fig. <xref rid="13256_2022_3333_Fig2_HTML" ref-type="fig">2</xref>), suggested the possibility of diseases involving cerebral white matter, such as posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) or PML. To confirm the diagnosis, the patient underwent a more accurate workup. The combination of clinical and radiological findings, along with positive polymerase chain reaction (PCR) for JCV in a cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) sample, confirmed the PML diagnosis. Other tests that were performed on the CSF sample, including glucose, protein, and inflammatory cell counts, were reported to be normal. To determine the risk factors for PML, other than PID, in the patient, human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) Ribonucleic acid (RNA) Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) testing was performed, which was reported negative. In general, the patient’s PID can be considered the most important risk factor for PML. Despite the treatments, our patient died within a month of diagnosis. He was the first case reported with PML due to mutations in the ), suggested the possibility of diseases involving cerebral white matter, such as posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) or PML. Other tests that were performed on the CSF sample, including glucose, protein, and inflammatory cell counts, were reported to be normal. To determine the risk factors for PML, other than PID, in the patient, human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) Ribonucleic acid (RNA) Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) testing was performed, which was reported negative. relevant_image_context: About 4 months ago, the patient was first diagnosed with nausea and headache, which gradually worsened along with progressive blurred vision, paresis, and paresthesia on the left side of the body. On brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), subcortical lesions in the left occipital and right temporoparietal area (Fig. 1), as well as the involvement of parts of the corpus callosum and thalamus, suggested the possibility of diseases involving cerebral white matter, such as posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) or PML. To confirm the diagnosis, the patient underwent a more accurate workup. The combination of clinical and radiological findings, along with positive polymerase chain reaction (PCR) for JCV in a cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) sample, confirmed the PML diagnosis. Other tests that were performed on the CSF sample, including glucose, protein, and inflammatory cell counts, were reported to be normal. To determine the risk factors for PML, other than PID, in the patient, HIV RNA PCR testing was performed, which was reported negative. In general, the patient’s PID can be considered the most important risk factor for PML. Despite the treatments, our patient died within a month of diagnosis. He was the first case reported with PML due to mutations in the RAC2 gene.
